narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background, your law school application was carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which looks at patterns from thousands of past students to predict bar exam success on the first try. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 3, a Year 3 decile of 2, an undergraduate GPA of 2.10, an LSAT score of 31, and plans to attend full-time—solid details that paint a picture of your academic journey so far. The model estimated a 61.7% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for admission, leading to the regrettable rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower Year 3 deciles, which had the strongest influence in pushing the model toward forecasting failure, as they stood out well below the typical student's mid-range performance around the 5th or 6th decile. Your Year 1 deciles also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about consistent academic standing compared to peers. On a more positive note, your upper-middle family income helped pull the prediction toward success, aligning favorably with many admitted students. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA, LSAT score, and full-time enrollment status each nudged things slightly in the direction of passing, though not enough to offset the top concerns. Overall, your grades in later school years placed you below the average for successful bar takers, who tend to rank higher in their deciles and carry stronger GPAs and LSATs into law school. While your background as a white male from an upper-middle-income family is common among applicants and didn't sway things negatively, the model prioritizes proven academic momentum for bar passage. We truly appreciate the effort you've put in and understand this news may feel disappointing. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT for a higher score, boosting your GPA through post-bac courses, or gaining experience that highlights upward trends in your performance—these steps have turned predictions around for many students like you. We're rooting for your success and encourage you to reapply once you've addressed these key areas.
